🇮🇳❤️ At 94, she has just one final wish.

Not wealth.
Not comfort.
Not recognition.
Just to be an Indian again.

Kondragunta Mahalakshmamma gave up her Indian citizenship in 2000 after becoming a U.S. citizen while living with her son in America.

Years later, despite having the opportunity to remain abroad, her heart never left the country where she was born.

In 2018, she returned to India.

And now, at the age of 94, she has formally applied to regain her Indian citizenship.

Her reason is deeply moving.

She says she wants to spend her remaining years in her homeland and wishes to "die as an Indian."

Recently, she met the Bapatla District Collector, requesting that her application be processed as soon as possible so she could live her final years in India and have her last rites performed in her native village.

For many, home is more than a place on a map.

It is the soil that raised you.

The language that shaped you.

The memories that never leave you.

Mahalakshmamma's decision is a reminder that while people may travel across the world and build lives in distant lands, the bond with one's roots often remains unbreakable.

Because sometimes, after a lifetime of journeys...

The heart simply wants to come home.

🇮🇳❤️

Follow CA Ammy Sharma

comment: Stories like this also highlight a policy challenge many countries face: citizenship is legal status, but belonging is often emotional and doesn't fit neatly into administrative timelines.
